courses.name: Web Programming
courses.description: The course aims to provide knowledge of practical and conceptual aspects of creating web pages and web services with interactive content. After completing the course, the student should be able to: Use and explain technologies such as HTML, CSS, Javascript, Python, Flask, SQL and JSON to capture interactive web content and, where appropriate, make connections to databases. Formulate and construct a "Single Page Applications". Explain the principles of web services and use simpler techniques to create and call web services.

detail.title: Web Programming, 6 credits
detail.name: Web Programming
detail.description: The course aims to provide knowledge of practical and conceptual aspects of creating web pages and web services with interactive content. After completing the course, the student should be able to: Use and explain technologies such as HTML, CSS, Javascript, Python, Flask, SQL and JSON to capture interactive web content and, where appropriate, make connections to databases. Formulate and construct a "Single Page Applications". Explain the principles of web services and use simpler techniques to create and call web services.
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 48 h Recommended self-study hours: 112 h
detail.prerequisites: Good knowledge of object-oriented programming for example Python or Java. Basic familiarity with web browsers and the Internet is assumed.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Biomedical Engineering Bachelor of Science in Computer Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Cybersecurity
detail.specific_information: Can not be included in the degree together with TDDD83.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The course aims to provide knowledge of practical and conceptual aspects of creating web pages and web services with interactive content. After completing the course, the student should be able to: Use and explain technologies such as HTML, CSS, Javascript, Python, Flask, SQL and JSON to capture interactive web content and, where appropriate, make connections to databases. Formulate and construct a "Single Page Applications". Explain the principles of web services and use simpler techniques to create and call web services.
detail.course_content: The course covers the following areas: Overview of WWW, HTML, Javascript and other client-server techniques. Programming languages Python, Flask, SQL, Websockets, JSON and other server-side technologies The course is based on a series of laboratories where the students independently solve tasks of varying scope and degree of difficulty. The students are expected to be able to independently interpret and carry out programming tasks in the field and to be able to download, install, configure and debug the software used in the laboratories. Great emphasis is placed on creating a holistic view of web programming where problem solving is a central part. The laboratory tasks include independently searching for, assessing, assimilating and applying information about new techniques. The students must use the assigned version management system for the code they develop during the course and continuously check in code during the laboratory work (in accordance with a professional way of working)
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course will consist of a number of lectures and a series of laboratory exercises.
detail.examination: LAB1 Project and laboratory work 6 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Machine Learning
courses.description: The overall aim of the course is to provide an introduction to machine learning, with special focus on regression and classification problems. Machine learning is presented from a probabilistic perspective with inference and prediction based on probability models. The course aims to give students an overview of machine learning within a unified framework and a good basis for further studies in the field. After completing the course the student should be able to: use relevant concepts and methods in machine learning to formulate, structure and solve practical problems. infer the parameters in a number of common machine learning models. use machine learning models for prediction and decision making. evaluate and choose among models. implement machine learning models and algorithms in a programming language.
curriculum_notes: For students who must take other compulsory courses in the scheduled block, this course is taken instead in semester 3.

After completing the course the student should be able to: use relevant concepts and methods in machine learning to formulate, structure and solve practical problems. infer the parameters in a number of common machine learning models. use machine learning models for prediction and decision making. evaluate and choose among models. implement machine learning models and algorithms in a programming language.
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 48 h Recommended self-study hours: 112 h Possible deviation from course syllabus/programme syllabus
detail.prerequisites: Probability theory; Statistics; Mathematical analysis; Linear Algebra; Basic programming.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management - International Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management Master of Science in Biomedical Engineering Bachelor's Programme in Mathematics Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Cybersecurity Master's Programme in Data Science and Information Engineering
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The overall aim of the course is to provide an introduction to machine learning, with special focus on regression and classification problems. Machine learning is presented from a probabilistic perspective with inference and prediction based on probability models. The course aims to give students an overview of machine learning within a unified framework and a good basis for further studies in the field. After completing the course the student should be able to: use relevant concepts and methods in machine learning to formulate, structure and solve practical problems. infer the parameters in a number of common machine learning models. use machine learning models for prediction and decision making. evaluate and choose among models. implement machine learning models and algorithms in a programming language.
detail.course_content: Introduction and overview of machine learning and its applications. Unsupervised and supervised learning. Discriminative and generative models. Prediction. Generalization. Classification. Nearest neighbors. Naïve Bayes. Discriminant analysis. Cross-validation. Model selection. Overfitting. Bootstrap. Regression. Regularization. Ridge regression. Lasso. Variable Selection. Binary and multi-class regression. Dimension reduction. PCA. ICA. Kernel smoothers. Support Vector Machines. Decision trees. Gaussian processes. Mixture models.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures and computer laboratory work. The lectures introduce concepts and theories that students then use in problem solving at the computer labs.
detail.examination: LAB2 Laboratory work 2 credits U, G DAT2 Computer examination 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 DAT2 is an exam in a computer hall that tests students' theoretical knowledge and problem-solving skills in machine learning. LAB2 consists of computer exercises that tests the students' ability to translate theoretical knowledge into practical problem solving in machine learning. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: AI Robotics
courses.description: The aim of this course is to give an overview of the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques for robotic systems, through the use of simulated robots and widely used software packages, such as the Robot Operating System (ROS). The main focus of the course is for students to learn how the different components that constitute a robot -- perception, control and deliberation – interact with each other to form an autonomous system. The course will have an emphasis on how such a system makes decisions to accomplish its goals. After the course, the student will be able to: to list and explain important problems and techniques in the area of AI robotics, to use existing frameworks to develop an autonomous robot in simulation, and to design, implement and evaluate algorithms needed to provide autonomous functionality to a robot in a simulated environment
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

detail.course_content: Perception and Scene Interpretation. Navigation: Localisation and path planning Autonomy and Levels of autonomy. Control and Decision-Making. Behavior-based robotics. Robotic Programming. Reactive, Deliberative and Hybrid robot architectures. Human-Robot Interraction
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: Through a serie of labs (~1 month), the students develop/integrate basic robotic functionality, so that in the end, it is a system that can move, avoid obstacle and take basic decision. The system will be improved during the project phase. Each student pick a topic related to AI Robotic (among a selected list of topics), implement and evaluate the algorithm, and write a report, with a description of the algorithm. Students are expected to present their individual work during a seminar and during a group seminar they will present their robotic system. During a lab session, they should demonstrate to the assistant the functionalities of their robot. Students work in groups of 5-6. The course runs over the entire spring semester.
detail.examination: PRA1 Project assignment 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laboratory work 2 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.
detail.other_information: Supplementary courses: Automated Planning, Sensor fusion, Computer Vision, Control Theory, Multi-Agents.

offerings.name: AI Robotics
offerings.description: The aim of this course is to give an overview of the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques for robotic systems, through the use of simulated robots and widely used software packages, such as the Robot Operating System (ROS). The main focus of the course is for students to learn how the different components that constitute a robot -- perception, control and deliberation – interact with each other to form an autonomous system. The course will have an emphasis on how such a system makes decisions to accomplish its goals. After the course, the student will be able to: to list and explain important problems and techniques in the area of AI robotics, to use existing frameworks to develop an autonomous robot in simulation, and to design, implement and evaluate algorithms needed to provide autonomous functionality to a robot in a simulated environment
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

courses.name: Bayesian Learning
courses.description: The course gives a solid introduction to Bayesian learning, with special emphasis on theory, models and methods used in machine learning applications. The student will learn about the basic ideas and concepts in Bayesian analysis from detailed analysis of simple probability models. The course presents simulation algorithms typically used in practical Bayesian work, and course participants will learn how to apply those algorithms to analyze complex machine learning models. After completing the course the student should be able to: derive the posterior distribution for a number of basic probability models use simulation algorithms to perform a Bayesian analysis of more complex models perform Bayesian prediction and decision making perform Bayesian model inference.

detail.title: Bayesian Learning, 6 credits
detail.name: Bayesian Learning
detail.description: The course gives a solid introduction to Bayesian learning, with special emphasis on theory, models and methods used in machine learning applications. The student will learn about the basic ideas and concepts in Bayesian analysis from detailed analysis of simple probability models. The course presents simulation algorithms typically used in practical Bayesian work, and course participants will learn how to apply those algorithms to analyze complex machine learning models. After completing the course the student should be able to: derive the posterior distribution for a number of basic probability models use simulation algorithms to perform a Bayesian analysis of more complex models perform Bayesian prediction and decision making perform Bayesian model inference.
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 48 h Recommended self-study hours: 112 h
detail.prerequisites: Mathematical analysis; Linear Algebra; Probability and Statistics; Machine Learning; Basic programming.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management - International Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Mathematics Master's Programme in Data Science and Information Engineering Master of Science in Engineering Mathematics
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The course gives a solid introduction to Bayesian learning, with special emphasis on theory, models and methods used in machine learning applications. The student will learn about the basic ideas and concepts in Bayesian analysis from detailed analysis of simple probability models. The course presents simulation algorithms typically used in practical Bayesian work, and course participants will learn how to apply those algorithms to analyze complex machine learning models. After completing the course the student should be able to: derive the posterior distribution for a number of basic probability models use simulation algorithms to perform a Bayesian analysis of more complex models perform Bayesian prediction and decision making perform Bayesian model inference.
detail.course_content: Likelihood, Subjective probability, Bayes theorem, Prior and posterior distribution, Bayesian analysis of the following models: Bernoulli, Normal, Multinomial, Multivariate normal, Linear and nonlinear regression, Binary regression, Mixture models; Regularization priors, Classification, Naïve Bayes, Marginalization, Posterior approximation, Prediction, Decision theory, Markov Chain Monte Carlo, Gibbs sampling, Bayesian variable selection, Model selection, Model averaging.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures, exercises, seminars and computer labs. The lectures introduce concepts and theories that students then use in problem solving at the exercises and computer labs. The seminars are used for student presentations of the computer lab reports and discussions.
detail.examination: LAB1 Computer assignments 1 credits U, G DAT2 Computer examination 5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 DAT2 is an exam in a computer hall that tests students' theoretical knowledge and problem-solving skills in Bayesian learning. LAB1 consists of computer exercises that tests the students' ability to translate theoretical knowledge into practical problem solving in Bayesian learning. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.
detail.other_information: Supplementary courses: Advanced Machine Learning, Text Mining, Visual Object Recognition and Detection

courses.name: Natural Language Processing
courses.description: Natural Language Processing (NLP) enables computers to work with human language. In this course, you will explore the core models and algorithms that underpin modern NLP, with a strong emphasis on practical experience. The course focuses particularly on large language models (LLMs) and the development of chatbots. On completion of the course, the student should be able to: explain central concepts, models and algorithms of NLP implement NLP algorithms and apply them to realistic problems evaluate NLP components and systems with appropriate methods identify, assess and make use of NLP research literature

detail.title: Natural Language Processing, 6 credits

detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 42 h Recommended self-study hours: 118 h
detail.prerequisites: discrete mathematics, calculus, linear algebra, probability theory good knowledge of programming, data structures, and algorithms machine learning (such as TDDE01 or TBMI26) The lab series for the course uses Python and the PyTorch library.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Data Science and Information Engineering Master of Science in Engineering Mathematics

detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is given through lectures, seminars, computer labs, and supervision in connection with a project. The lecture part of the course has a flipped classroom format.
detail.examination: UPG3 Theory assignments 1.5 credits U, G LAB3 Laboratory assignments 1.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 UPG4 Project assignments (group part) 1.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 UPG5 Project assignments (individual part) 1.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 The final grade for the course is the weighted average of the grades awarded for LAB3 (50%), UPG4 (20%) and UPG5 (30%). This value is rounded to the nearest integer; halfway values are rounded up. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Multi Agent Systems
courses.description: The overall aim of the course is to give an overview of multiagent systems and in depth knowledge of some areas of multiagent systems. After the course students should be able to: List and explain important problems and techniques in the area of multiagent systems. Explain how modern multiagent systems based on large language models work and can be used. Explain how central algorithms in the area of multiagent systems work. Be able to implement some central algorithm in the area of multiagent systems. Evaluate and apply different game theoretic approaches. Design and use auctions for allocating resources in a multiagent system. Model relevant aspects of multiagent system decision making using markov decision processes and logics.

detail.course_content: Architectures for multiagent systems Modern agent systems based on large language models Distributed AI, including distributed constraint satisfaction and optimization Game theory, including normal form and extensive form games Communication, including speech acts Aggregated preferences, including voting Auctions for multiagent resource allocation Multiagent decision-making, including task allocation
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: Lectures, seminars and labs. Lab assignments will be used to learn more about the practical aspects of multiagent systems and explore some techniques in more detail. The examination will mainly be homework exercises.
detail.examination: UPG1 Assignments 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laboratory work 2 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Advanced Machine Learning
courses.description: The course presents the analysis of several large classes of models widely used in advanced machine learning, such as state-space models, gaussian processes, hidden Markov models, Bayesian networks, and Markov random fields. Students will learn about the structure and learning of these models, when they are applicable, how to use them in practical machine learning applications, and how to correctly interpret the results. The models are mainly analyzed from a Bayesian perspective. After completing the course, the student should be able to: use the introduced model classes to accurately formulate and solve practical problems. learn the parameters and perform predictions in the presented models. devise algorithms to make an agent learn how to act optimally by interacting with the environment, and implement the models and learning methods in a programming language.

detail.course_content: ​The course covers advanced methods in machine learning that allow for modelling complex phenomena and predicting the outcomes of these phenomena. The following topics are included in the course: probabilistic graphical models, such as Bayesian networks and Markov random fields, hidden Markov models including the forward-backward and Viterbi algorithms, reinforcement learning including the Q-learning and REINFORCE algorithms, Gaussian process for classification and regression.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures, seminars and computer laboratory work. The lectures introduce concepts and theories that students then use in problem solving at the computer labs. Seminars comprise student presentations and discussion of computer lab reports.

LAB1 consists of computer exercises that tests the students' ability to translate theoretical knowledge into practical problem solving in machine learning. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Text Mining
courses.description: Text Mining develops methods for accessing information in and extracting knowledge from large volumes of text. The overall aim of this course is to provide students with practical experience of the main steps of text mining: information retrieval, processing of text data, modelling, analysis of experimental results. The course ends with an individual project where students work on a self-defined problem. On completion of the course, the student should be able to: implement text mining methods and apply them to practical problems analyse and summarise results from text mining experiments identify, formulate and solve problems within the area of text mining clearly present and discuss the conclusions of a project work

detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 24 h Recommended self-study hours: 136 h
detail.prerequisites: discrete mathematics, calculus, linear algebra, probability theory programming, data structures and algorithms machine learning The lab series for the course uses Python.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: Text Mining develops methods for accessing information in and extracting knowledge from large volumes of text. The overall aim of this course is to provide students with practical experience of the main steps of text mining: information retrieval, processing of text data, modelling, analysis of experimental results. The course ends with an individual project where students work on a self-defined problem. On completion of the course, the student should be able to: implement text mining methods and apply them to practical problems analyse and summarise results from text mining experiments identify, formulate and solve problems within the area of text mining clearly present and discuss the conclusions of a project work
detail.course_content: information retrieval basic natural language processing predictive modelling, in particular text classification text clustering and topic modelling information extraction validation methods applications of text mining software libraries and data
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is given in the form of lectures, computer labs, and supervision in connection with an individual project.
detail.examination: PRA1 Project 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laboratory exercises 3 credits U, G The final grade for the course is the grade awarded for PRA1. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

detail.title: Programming C++, 6 credits
detail.name: Programming C++
detail.description: After the course, the student should be able to: use basic language constructs and the language's standard library use design principles, methods and techniques used in object-oriented programming to construct programs that solve given problems
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 110 h Recommended self-study hours: 50 h
detail.prerequisites: Skill in one imperative, object-oriented or functional programming language.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Chemical Biology Master of Science in Mechanical Engineering Master of Science in Engineering Biology Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Energy - Environment - Management Master of Science in Design and Product Development Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management Master's Programme in Mechanical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Cybersecurity
detail.specific_information: Can not be included in the degree together with TDDD33 or TDDE79.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After the course, the student should be able to: use basic language constructs and the language's standard library use design principles, methods and techniques used in object-oriented programming to construct programs that solve given problems
detail.course_content: Programming: Creating algorithms and problem solving. Syntax: C++ language features including control statements, operators and types, references, pointers, exceptions, classes, functions, dynamic memory, inheritance, polymorphism and the standard template library. Tools: Linux terminal based programming environment with program editor, compiler, debugger and versioning system.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of: A set of lectures introducing each of the areas of C++ language that we cover. Participating in lectures, lessons and labs is essential to gain all course content. A set of computer laborations performed in pairs. Self studies in order to prepare and complete each laboration on time. A final individual computer exam with problems covering the topics discussed at lectures, lessons and labs. The course runs over the entire autumn semester.
detail.examination: UPG1 Programming assignments 4 credits U, G DAT1 Computer examination 2 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Advanced Project Course - AI and Machine Learning
courses.description: The project should have significant technical level that requires in-depth subject knowledge in artificial intelligence and machine learning, should be carried out in a professional manner, and should develop and consolidate the participants' skills in the following areas: Analyze and structure problems in the area of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Apply knowledge and methods from a wide range of previous courses in the areas of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Independently acquire new knowledge, as required by the project. Integrate knowledge from many disciplines and apply them in the context of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Formulate a requirement specification for the project based on a project directive and thereby assess the feasibility of the project in terms of technical solutions and available resources. Present the project results for teh client as well as for other students, which can not be presumed to be specialists in the techniques used. Actively contribute to a well functioning project group. Demonstrate the ability to lead the project work with the support of a project model, and with limited access to supervisory resources. Plan, implement and monitor a project in the area of artificial intelligence and machine learning. The result of the project work should: Attain high technical quality and be based on modern knowledge and practices in the relevant field of technology. Be documented in relevant project documents and relevant technical documentation. Be presented orally. Meet the requirements stated in the specification.
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

detail.name: Advanced Project Course - AI and Machine Learning
detail.description: The project should have significant technical level that requires in-depth subject knowledge in artificial intelligence and machine learning, should be carried out in a professional manner, and should develop and consolidate the participants' skills in the following areas: Analyze and structure problems in the area of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Apply knowledge and methods from a wide range of previous courses in the areas of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Independently acquire new knowledge, as required by the project. Integrate knowledge from many disciplines and apply them in the context of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Formulate a requirement specification for the project based on a project directive and thereby assess the feasibility of the project in terms of technical solutions and available resources. Present the project results for teh client as well as for other students, which can not be presumed to be specialists in the techniques used. Actively contribute to a well functioning project group. Demonstrate the ability to lead the project work with the support of a project model, and with limited access to supervisory resources. Plan, implement and monitor a project in the area of artificial intelligence and machine learning. The result of the project work should: Attain high technical quality and be based on modern knowledge and practices in the relevant field of technology. Be documented in relevant project documents and relevant technical documentation. Be presented orally. Meet the requirements stated in the specification.
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 64 h Recommended self-study hours: 96 h Possible deviation from course syllabus/programme syllabus
detail.prerequisites: The course expects the student to have applied project management models in previous courses or other context. The student should also have acquired knowledge equivalent to basic courses in the profile "AI and machine learning" or the specialization "AI and data mining" in the area covered by the project.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The project should have significant technical level that requires in-depth subject knowledge in artificial intelligence and machine learning, should be carried out in a professional manner, and should develop and consolidate the participants' skills in the following areas: Analyze and structure problems in the area of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Apply knowledge and methods from a wide range of previous courses in the areas of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Independently acquire new knowledge, as required by the project. Integrate knowledge from many disciplines and apply them in the context of artificial intelligence and machine learning. Formulate a requirement specification for the project based on a project directive and thereby assess the feasibility of the project in terms of technical solutions and available resources. Present the project results for teh client as well as for other students, which can not be presumed to be specialists in the techniques used. Actively contribute to a well functioning project group. Demonstrate the ability to lead the project work with the support of a project model, and with limited access to supervisory resources. Plan, implement and monitor a project in the area of artificial intelligence and machine learning. The result of the project work should: Attain high technical quality and be based on modern knowledge and practices in the relevant field of technology. Be documented in relevant project documents and relevant technical documentation. Be presented orally. Meet the requirements stated in the specification.
detail.course_content: Description of the projects, with project directives, are available on the course website. The projects will be closely linked to either ongoing research within the field of computer science or to companies active in this field. Examples could be develop a robotic system to perform some specific type of tasks, develop a system that learns to detect and track objects from sensor data, develop a recommender system for a specific domain, develop a system that learns to predict the activity of an object based on prior observations. The nature of the projects may change from year to year.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The project, which is formed according to directive given later, should consist of at least six students. Each group will be assigned a supervisor, who will support the group in its work and answer technical questions. For each project, there is a client with whom the project team negotiates a specification. Before project work begins, the project team should create appropriate project management documents for the project. For each instance of the course, the examiner will present a set of project proposals. Assignment of projects to student groups is based both on their aptitude and their wishes. For each proposal there is a project charter forming the basis for further work. The project begins with the project team developing a requirements specification and relevant project management documentation for their project. The projects should be conducted according to an appropriate development model, selected by the team. The course runs over the entire autymn semester.
detail.examination: PRA1 Project 6 credits U, G The project work will be assessed on the achievement of course objectives. Three modules, each assessed with pass/fail, are included in the assessment. These topics are: Technical level and quality of project results Written documentation in the form of technical report and relevant project documents Oral presentation To pass the whole project work, the student is required to pass all parts and meet the objectives of the course. Special emphasis is given to participants actively contributing to the group working according to the project model's intentions. Grades are given as ”Fail” or ”Pass”. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.
detail.grades: Two grade scale, older version, U, G

curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

offerings.name: Advanced Project Course: Secure Distributed and Embedded Systems
offerings.description: The project should have significant technical level that requires in-depth subject knowledge in at least one of the subjects networks, distributed systems, security or embedded systems. The project should be carried out in a professional manner, and should develop and consolidate the participants' skills in the area of specialization. Analyze and structure problems in the area of specialization. Apply knowledge and methods from a wide range of previous courses in the area of specialization. Independently acquire new knowledge, as required by the project. Integrate knowledge from many disciplines and apply them in the context of the area of specialization. Formulate a requirement specification for the project based on a project directive and thereby assess the feasibility of the project in terms of technical solutions and available resources. Present the project results for the client as well as for other students, which can not be presumed to be specialists in the techniques used. Actively contribute to a well functioning project group. Demonstrate the ability to lead the project work with the support of a project model, and with limited access to supervisory resources. Plan, implement and monitor a project in the area of specialization. The result of the project work should: Attain high technical quality and be based on modern knowledge and practices in the relevant field of technology. Be documented in relevant project documents and relevant technical documentation. Be presented orally. Meet the requirements stated in the specification.
offerings.specialisation: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

courses.name: Software Verification
courses.description: The course is an introduction to the theory and practice of software verification. After completion of the course, students will be able to: Demonstrate knowledge about the principals behind software verification approaches including Hoare-style axiomatic reasoning, satisfiability modulo theory and abstract interpretation. Apply existing techniques in order to analyse and verify software. Describe advantages, limitations and research challenges of software verification.

detail.title: Software Design and Construction, 6 credits
detail.name: Software Design and Construction
detail.description: After the course, students shall be able to: analyze the structure and organization in a larger program critically evaluate design principles and -patterns in relation to desired properties of software and interfaces design a program that handles internationalization and localization apply tools that help the user to refactor or generate source-code apply different forms of metaprogramming apply methods and tools to find different kinds of problems in source code both before and during execution of a program, for example parallel execution or memory leaks
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 40 h Recommended self-study hours: 120 h
detail.prerequisites: Basic course in software engineering (including UML) Ability to program in multiple programming languages (including object-oriented programming and pointers / memory management) Data structures The student should know how to read research papers on software engineering and search information independently on new topics.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management - International Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science

detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After the course, students shall be able to: analyze the structure and organization in a larger program critically evaluate design principles and -patterns in relation to desired properties of software and interfaces design a program that handles internationalization and localization apply tools that help the user to refactor or generate source-code apply different forms of metaprogramming apply methods and tools to find different kinds of problems in source code both before and during execution of a program, for example parallel execution or memory leaks
detail.course_content: History of design patterns. Design patterns. Refactoring. Use of integrated development environments. Code generation. Design for testability. Design of domain-specific languages. Metaprogramming. Dependency injection. Design of frameworks and API:s. Advanced debugging of memory allocation in parallel programs. Memory profiling.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: Students use online lecture material and study course literature as preparations for lab assignments in pairs and seminars in groups, where issues concerning the course literature are treated. The lab assignments differ in scope and depth: some train students in basic skills and techniques with some guidance, whereas other assignments have students develop and analyze larger programmes with less guidance.
detail.examination: DAT1 Computer-based exam 2 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laboratory work 3 credits U, G UPG1 Assignment 1 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

detail.title: Methods and Tools for Large Distributed Projects, 6 credits
detail.name: Methods and Tools for Large Distributed Projects
detail.description: After this course, students shall be able to: Identify, categorize and assess different roles in a large-scale development project (UPG1) Describe and analyze the organization, requirements and coding conventions of a large-scale development project (UPG1) Use, adapt, and develop advanced tools and methods appropriate for large-scale development projects (LAB1) Analyze methods and tools for large-scale development within the context of a specific development project.

detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 30 h Recommended self-study hours: 130 h
detail.prerequisites: Proficient in unit-level testing and software design principles and patterns. Also have basic proficiency in source code management tools and build tools for software. Proficient in reading technical English texts.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science

Principles for distributed version control systems such as, for example, Git and Mercurial.

Individually assessed orally at the end of the course for final grade. There will be three opportunities per year to be assessed in the course, during exam periods. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Programming Project with Open Source Code
courses.description: After the course, students shall be able to: Use existing conventions and follow established processes to contribute through software to an open source project. Present changes and updates so external parties may approve submissions. Create a time plan and monitor progress through a common development project Use appropriate tools for contemporary, large-scale software development Independently acquire new knowledge and skills in order to contribute to a large-scale software project.
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

detail.title: Programming Project with Open Source Code, 6 credits
detail.name: Programming Project with Open Source Code
detail.description: After the course, students shall be able to: Use existing conventions and follow established processes to contribute through software to an open source project. Present changes and updates so external parties may approve submissions. Create a time plan and monitor progress through a common development project Use appropriate tools for contemporary, large-scale software development Independently acquire new knowledge and skills in order to contribute to a large-scale software project.
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 0 h Recommended self-study hours: 160 h
detail.prerequisites: Students from the swedish programme “Civilingenjör” are expected to have acquired knowledge corresponding to courses in one of the two profiles “Large Scale Software Engineering” or “Programming and Algorithms” depending on which focus area in the course the student chooses. In particular, for the “Large Scale Software Engineering” profile it is recommended to have acquired the knowledge from Methods and tools for large distributed projects. Students from the international Master's programme in Computer Science are expected to have acquired knowledge corresponding to courses in the specialization “Programming and Software Methods”.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After the course, students shall be able to: Use existing conventions and follow established processes to contribute through software to an open source project. Present changes and updates so external parties may approve submissions. Create a time plan and monitor progress through a common development project Use appropriate tools for contemporary, large-scale software development Independently acquire new knowledge and skills in order to contribute to a large-scale software project.
detail.course_content: The course is based on development in a large open source project to which the students contribute. The students plan and perform their own contributions to the project, and also support each others efforts. The students are given the opportunity to focus their work in one of two directions. The first direction is on large scale projects, which puts high demands on the development process, communication with the project and planning. This direction is based on the profile Large Scale Software Engineering. The other direction is on algorithm development where the code contribution should be more advanced. This is often possible only in medium-sized projects. This direction build on the profile Programming and Algorithms. Both directions in the course requires planning, cooperation and non-trivial code contributions.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: Students work during the entire autumn semester by contributing to open source projects. The students choose which project to work with and meet regularly in teams of 6-8 people together with teachers who guide and support the students in their work. The students are also responsible to contribute to the collective contributions in the team become as good as possible by actively working together with the other team members. At the end of the semester the contributions are presented in an open seminar.
detail.examination: PRA1 Project 6 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Projects are assessed orally and individually. At the start of the course, specific criteria for assessing project contributions according to course goals are presented. During the oral examination, activity traces from collaboration platforms such as Gitlab or Github are used as a basis for assessment. Artifacts to be reviewed include individual code changes contributed, reviews of other team members' submissions, internal team support, time planning and review. Individual changes are reviewed based on acceptance in external projects, as well as course staff criteria, including assessment of the technical level of difficulty, adherence to good industrial practice and design. Re-examination is conducted during exam periods. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Wireless Connectivity
courses.description: The students that will take that course will be equipped with knowledge that will allow them to critically identify, reason, and also design future communication networks. After the course the student will be able to: Understand the fundamentals for communicating over a shared wireless medium Understand different access control mechanisms, and characterize their performance tradeoffs Understand and analyze important communication networking metrics, such as latency, throughput, reliability, the freshness of information Understand the scalability issues of wireless communication networks and identify interference mitigation techniques Simulate specific elements of communications networks and give insights on the performance, argue on the accuracy of the theoretical analysis Argue on the limitations of the current approaches/technologies Understand some new directions in 5G and beyond communication systems such as communication design for distributed Machine Learning
specialisations: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security

detail.prerequisites: Probability and Statistics. Mathematical knowledge on basic information theoretic concepts and the time and frequency sharing ideas from for example “Signals, Information, Communication” is helpful but not a requirement.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science

detail.course_content: Introduction to the shared wireless medium Random access Layering and slicing Limits of information transmission over a wireless medium Timing and information freshness in wireless communications Space in wireless communications Wireless networking – beyond a link Large-scale communication networks Selected topics: communications for distributed Machine Learning, wireless network control systems or CPS, non-terrestrial networks, etc.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: Lectures, tutorials, labs and smaller project assignment. Guest lectures from industry and academia. The course lasts the enire spring semester.

courses.name: Information Security: Privacy, System and Network Security
courses.description: The course aims to provide deepened knowledge about both typical security threats against computer systems and networks, and about methods for mitigating such threats. The course also covers methods and techniques for ensuring user privacy in computer systems and networks. After passing the course, a student should be able to: explain and use the security terminology and security principles presented in the course in a given context, identify security weaknesses in a given system or network, identify, and explain in detail, the security threats against the system or network, suggest suitable technologies, methods, or design decisions for mitigating the security threats against the system or network explain and apply the techniques and methods covered in the course for ensuring user privacy using some selected technologies, design and implement solutions to given security problems, based on a set of security requirements.
specialisations: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security

detail.title: Information Security: Privacy, System and Network Security, 6 credits
detail.name: Information Security: Privacy, System and Network Security
detail.description: The course aims to provide deepened knowledge about both typical security threats against computer systems and networks, and about methods for mitigating such threats. The course also covers methods and techniques for ensuring user privacy in computer systems and networks. After passing the course, a student should be able to: explain and use the security terminology and security principles presented in the course in a given context, identify security weaknesses in a given system or network, identify, and explain in detail, the security threats against the system or network, suggest suitable technologies, methods, or design decisions for mitigating the security threats against the system or network explain and apply the techniques and methods covered in the course for ensuring user privacy using some selected technologies, design and implement solutions to given security problems, based on a set of security requirements.
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 46 h Recommended self-study hours: 114 h
detail.prerequisites: Basic course in computer security. Basic understanding of operating system concepts and TCP/IP networks is recommended.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Cybersecurity
detail.specific_information: The course can not be included in degree together with TDDD17
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The course aims to provide deepened knowledge about both typical security threats against computer systems and networks, and about methods for mitigating such threats. The course also covers methods and techniques for ensuring user privacy in computer systems and networks. After passing the course, a student should be able to: explain and use the security terminology and security principles presented in the course in a given context, identify security weaknesses in a given system or network, identify, and explain in detail, the security threats against the system or network, suggest suitable technologies, methods, or design decisions for mitigating the security threats against the system or network explain and apply the techniques and methods covered in the course for ensuring user privacy using some selected technologies, design and implement solutions to given security problems, based on a set of security requirements.
detail.course_content: Network security: Network design for security Weaknesses in, and attacks against, common network protocols and technologies Technologies for secure communication Technologies for detection and prevention of intrusions (firewalls, IDS, etc.) System security: Security architecture and access control in modern computer systems Attacks against hardware and system software Common techniques used in malware, and techniques for detection and defense against malware Privacy: Important terminology, definitions, and models within the area of privacy Techniques and methods for ensuring privacy in digital communication Privacy and anonymity in big data processing and databases
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures and laboratory work.
detail.examination: TEN1 Written examination 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laboratory work and written assignments 2 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Sports Analytics
courses.description: To gain an understanding of the research issues related to sports analytics To obtain knowledge about problems in sports analytics and algorithms for solving these problems To be able to use relevant algorithms in a sports analytics application

detail.title: Sports Analytics, 6 credits
detail.name: Sports Analytics
detail.description: To gain an understanding of the research issues related to sports analytics To obtain knowledge about problems in sports analytics and algorithms for solving these problems To be able to use relevant algorithms in a sports analytics application
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 0 h Recommended self-study hours: 160 h
detail.prerequisites: Machine Learning an/or Data mining and/or a course in AI. A course in Statistics.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: To gain an understanding of the research issues related to sports analytics To obtain knowledge about problems in sports analytics and algorithms for solving these problems To be able to use relevant algorithms in a sports analytics application
detail.course_content: Sports analytics deals with using data related to sports events to obtain insights about the sport and its surroundings. The insights can relate to such things as player and team performance, strategies, training, injuries, and rules of the game. Sports: - ice hockey - football - baseball - basketball - others based on student interest Sports analytics problems: - development and visualization of performance statistics - player, lineup and team valuation - player career trends - team management - team strategies - game event detection - injury detection and classification Techniques: - Machine learning - Image recognition - Visualization - Knowledge representation
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course comprises a lecture part and a project part. During the lecture part different research topics are discussed. Lectures are given by the teachers, guests as well as students. During the project part the students investigate a course-related topic of their choice under supervision of the teachers.
detail.examination: MUN1 Oral presentation of research paper 2 credits U, G PRA1 Project work 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Programming of Parallel Computers - Methods and Tools
courses.description: Parallel computers are used for heavy computations. The student should acquire knowledge about the programming of parallel computers and master selected methods and tools. The course shall also give an overview of how parallel computers can be used in some application areas, such as image analysis and scientific computations. After the course the student is expected to be able to 1) use efficient methods and languages for the programming of parallel computers, 2) program parallel computers with distributed memory (MPI) and shared memory (OpenMP).

detail.title: Programming of Parallel Computers - Methods and Tools, 6 credits
detail.name: Programming of Parallel Computers - Methods and Tools
detail.description: Parallel computers are used for heavy computations. The student should acquire knowledge about the programming of parallel computers and master selected methods and tools. The course shall also give an overview of how parallel computers can be used in some application areas, such as image analysis and scientific computations. After the course the student is expected to be able to 1) use efficient methods and languages for the programming of parallel computers, 2) program parallel computers with distributed memory (MPI) and shared memory (OpenMP).
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 52 h Recommended self-study hours: 108 h
detail.prerequisites: Basic course in programming. A course in process programming is useful but not required, since understanding the process concept is assumed. Programming skills in C/C++ are required.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management - International Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management Master's Programme in Electronics Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Mathematics
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: Parallel computers are used for heavy computations. The student should acquire knowledge about the programming of parallel computers and master selected methods and tools. The course shall also give an overview of how parallel computers can be used in some application areas, such as image analysis and scientific computations. After the course the student is expected to be able to 1) use efficient methods and languages for the programming of parallel computers, 2) program parallel computers with distributed memory (MPI) and shared memory (OpenMP).
detail.course_content: Parallel computer architecture: memory hierarchies, shared memory and distributed memory architectures. Vector instructions. Parallel execution models and programming languages. Performance measurements and enhancement. Message passing, multithreaded and dataparallel programming. Design and analysis of fundamental parallel algorithms. Time complexity. Scalability. Scheduling of parallel programs. Grid computing. Tools for parallel programming. MPI (Message passing interface) and OpenMP. Basic parallel algorithms and BLAS (Basic Linear Algebra Subprograms). Application areas. Parallel solving of equation systems. The laboratory course gives practical experience in programming parallel systems (different programming paradigms are used).
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The lectures deal with theory and principles, and the laboratory course gives practical exercise in parallel programming and the use of support systems. The lab course uses parallel supercomputer resources located at the National Supercomputer Center.
detail.examination: LAB1 Laboratory work 3 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 The questions in the written exam check how well the student has fulfilled the learning goals of the course. For passing the exam, deficits in fulfilling certain partial goals can be balanced by a better fulfilling of other partial goals. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Compiler Construction
courses.description: The aim of this course is to give a comprehensive introduction to the theoretical and practical issues underlying the design and implementation of compilers. After the completion of the course you should be able to: explain and apply fundamental principles and techniques of compiler design explain and use methods for lexical analysis, top-down and bottom-up parsing explain and use methods for semantic analysis, syntax-directed translation, code optimization explain methods and theory for construction of lexer and parser generators, code generator generators explain methods for generation and optimization of code for RISC-based architectures construct and implement a top-down parser and a bottom-up parser for a given context-free grammar use lexer and parser generators to build a lexical analyzer and a parser for a real compiler explain and use methods for machine code generation design and implement a complete compiler including: lexer, parser, memory management, semantic analysis, optimization, and machine code generation

detail.title: Compiler Construction, 6 credits
detail.name: Compiler Construction
detail.description: The aim of this course is to give a comprehensive introduction to the theoretical and practical issues underlying the design and implementation of compilers. After the completion of the course you should be able to: explain and apply fundamental principles and techniques of compiler design explain and use methods for lexical analysis, top-down and bottom-up parsing explain and use methods for semantic analysis, syntax-directed translation, code optimization explain methods and theory for construction of lexer and parser generators, code generator generators explain methods for generation and optimization of code for RISC-based architectures construct and implement a top-down parser and a bottom-up parser for a given context-free grammar use lexer and parser generators to build a lexical analyzer and a parser for a real compiler explain and use methods for machine code generation design and implement a complete compiler including: lexer, parser, memory management, semantic analysis, optimization, and machine code generation
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 48 h Recommended self-study hours: 112 h
detail.prerequisites: Data Structures and Algorithms. Formal Languages and Automata Theory, either via that course or studying selected material (check with lecturer). Some knowledge of C-based languages such as C++ is an advantage.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.specific_information: Can not be included in degree together with: TDDD55, TDDB44.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The aim of this course is to give a comprehensive introduction to the theoretical and practical issues underlying the design and implementation of compilers. After the completion of the course you should be able to: explain and apply fundamental principles and techniques of compiler design explain and use methods for lexical analysis, top-down and bottom-up parsing explain and use methods for semantic analysis, syntax-directed translation, code optimization explain methods and theory for construction of lexer and parser generators, code generator generators explain methods for generation and optimization of code for RISC-based architectures construct and implement a top-down parser and a bottom-up parser for a given context-free grammar use lexer and parser generators to build a lexical analyzer and a parser for a real compiler explain and use methods for machine code generation design and implement a complete compiler including: lexer, parser, memory management, semantic analysis, optimization, and machine code generation
detail.course_content: Different types of translators such as compilers and preprocessors. Methods for lexical analysis and syntax analysis. Management of declarations. Different types of internal representations. Memory management and run-time organization. Code generation and code optimization, especially with regards to RISC processors. Methods for handling errors. Compiler construction and compiler generation tools. Language design. A complete compiler for a Pascal-like language is constructed during the laboratory sessions. Certain modules of this compiler will be automatically generated using compiler generation tools, whereas other parts will be implemented by hand.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The theory is presented in the lectures. The seminars prepare for the laboratory assignments. In the labs a complete compiler for a small procedural language is implemented.
detail.examination: LAB1 Laboratory Work 3 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 The questions in the written exam check how well the student has fulfilled the learning goals of the course. For passing the exam, deficits in fulfilling certain partial goals can be balanced by a better fulfilling of other partial goals. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Concurrent Programming and Operating Systems
courses.description: After completing the course, the student shall be able to: Show basic knowledge of concepts, problems and methods for concurrent programming and operating systems. (LAB1 + TEN1) Identify concurrency problems and design solutions to these problems. (TEN1) Describe different requirements, functionality, and architectures for operating systems. (TEN1) Get acquainted with an existing operating system and design improvements according to set requirements. (LAB1) Implement, test and systematically debug complex system software. (LAB1)

detail.title: Concurrent Programming and Operating Systems, 6 credits
detail.name: Concurrent Programming and Operating Systems
detail.description: After completing the course, the student shall be able to: Show basic knowledge of concepts, problems and methods for concurrent programming and operating systems. (LAB1 + TEN1) Identify concurrency problems and design solutions to these problems. (TEN1) Describe different requirements, functionality, and architectures for operating systems. (TEN1) Get acquainted with an existing operating system and design improvements according to set requirements. (LAB1) Implement, test and systematically debug complex system software. (LAB1)
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 60 h Recommended self-study hours: 100 h
detail.prerequisites: Programming in C or C++. Data structures and algorithms. Computer hardware. Distributed version management (e.g., git).
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.specific_information: The course can not be included in degree together with TDDB68.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After completing the course, the student shall be able to: Show basic knowledge of concepts, problems and methods for concurrent programming and operating systems. (LAB1 + TEN1) Identify concurrency problems and design solutions to these problems. (TEN1) Describe different requirements, functionality, and architectures for operating systems. (TEN1) Get acquainted with an existing operating system and design improvements according to set requirements. (LAB1) Implement, test and systematically debug complex system software. (LAB1)
detail.course_content: The course is composed of two modules, concurrent programming and operating systems. The concurrent programming module contains: the process concept, mutual exclusion, mechanisms for synchronisation, process interaction and deadlocks. The operating system module contains: system calls, processes and thread, scheduling, memory management, file systems, and design principles for different kinds of operating systems such as cloud systems, mobile devices, and embedded devices.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: A series of lectures presents the theory. Laboratory assignments help to create applied knowledge of concurrency and operating system principles. Lessons prepare for the lab sessions.
detail.examination: UPG1 Voluntary lab assignment 0 credits U, G LAB1 Laboratory Work 3 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 UPG1 is marked as passed if the lab course is passed within the announced deadline and the participant is first-time registered on the course in the current year. The questions in the written exam check how well the student has fulfilled the learning goals of the course. For passing the exam, deficits in fulfilling certain partial goals can be balanced by a better fulfilling of other partial goals. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Deep Learning
courses.description: The overall aim of the course is to give the student a good theoretical understanding and good practical skills in machine learning based on deep neural networks. On completion of the course, the student should be able to: 1. Use central theoretical concepts to describe and account for differences between models and methods in deep learning 2. Use relevant models and methods from deep learning to formulate, structure and solve practical problems involving large and complex data sets 3. Choose appropriate evaluation protocols and critically evaluate the quality of the solutions 4. Analyze and criticize the choice of models and methods 5. Describe ethical issues and societal aspects related to deep learning.

detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 42 h Recommended self-study hours: 118 h Possible deviation from course syllabus/programme syllabus
detail.prerequisites: Calculus in one and several variables, linear algebra, statistics, optimization and programming. Basic machine learning (at least 6 credits) including artificial neural networks. Programming in Python and experience in programming for numerical problem solving and linear algebra (e.g. via NumPy, Matlab or Julia).
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Biomedical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master of Science in Engineering Mathematics
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The overall aim of the course is to give the student a good theoretical understanding and good practical skills in machine learning based on deep neural networks. On completion of the course, the student should be able to: 1. Use central theoretical concepts to describe and account for differences between models and methods in deep learning 2. Use relevant models and methods from deep learning to formulate, structure and solve practical problems involving large and complex data sets 3. Choose appropriate evaluation protocols and critically evaluate the quality of the solutions 4. Analyze and criticize the choice of models and methods 5. Describe ethical issues and societal aspects related to deep learning.
detail.course_content: The course introduces key concepts in deep learning, from basic to state-of-the-art. This includes: Common model types (e.g., MLP, CNN, RNN, Transformers); Optimization and learning algorithms for deep networks; Representational learning and self-supervised learning; Generative models; Different application areas of deep learning, in e.g. natural language processing and text analysis, computer vision, and natural sciences; Ethical considerations and societal aspects linked to deep learning.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The teaching consists of lectures, computer labs, and project work with a subsequent seminar. Lectures are used to introduce concepts and theory that the students then use in practical problem solving in computer labs and in project work.
detail.examination: LAB1 Computer Based Laboratory Assignmnets 2 credits U, G DIT1 Digital Examination 2 credits U, 3, 4, 5 UPG1 Project Work 2 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 consists of computer labs that test the students' ability to translate theoretical knowledge into practical problem solving. DIT1 is a digital exam that tests the students' theoretical knowledge. UPG1 is a mini-project that tests the students' ability to translate theoretical knowledge into practical skills and to solve complex problems with deep learning. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Professionalism in Computer Science
courses.description: The aim of this course is to prepare students for academic studies at advanced level, as well as to teach an academic approach at large. The course will provide knowledge about computer science research, academic writing, sustainable development and ethics. It constitutes thus an opportunity to learn about conventions, regulations and expectations of advanced level studies and research at a university. In addition, it provides the student with tools to facilitate a transition from consumer of science to producer of science. Upon completion of the course, the student will be able to: Academic writing understand and produce an outline of a technical and scientific report summarize scientific publications understand and apply the requirements for source references and reference management in written reports employ basic principles of review of written reports Sustainable Development present and discuss economic, social and ecological fundamentals in sustainable development identify and describe examples of sustainability aspects related to the field of computer science explain and discuss the relevance of computer technology for sustainable development compare and evaluate computer science's possibilities and constraints in society as well as peoples’ responsibility for how they are used in a sustainability perspective reflect on and critically review sustainability and sustainable methods in computer science analyze the consequences of computer technology applications on sustainable development and suggest system designs that take these consequences into account Ethics account for some common ethical theories, principles and concepts as well as apply them to specific cases and situations present and discuss ethical challenges related to computer technology development and use demonstrate insight into the role of knowledge in society and on people's responsibility for how it is used demonstrate the ability to make judgments in computer science with regard to relevant ethical aspects
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods
