courses.name: Computer Networks
courses.description: After the course, you are expected to be able to: Explain, describe, and analyze a typical network architecture, including the importance of network layers and encapsulation Explain the different basic types of protocols, communication channels, and network types Design, implement, verify, and test your own protocols Explain fundamental performance tradeoffs, including showing an understanding of where delays can occur in a network, what different types of delay that exist, the impact of packet losses and jitter on various protocols Overall, you should have an applied understanding of the network architecture and the protocols associated with the different layers, as well as how they are implemented: Describe and analyze the most common application architectures in the Internet, how the most important application-layer protocols work, the service they provide, as well as have the ability to design and implement their own application-layer protocols Analyze and explain important design considerations at the transport layer, including hands-on knowledge of how flow control and congestion control works, and how reliable data transfer is implemented Motivate and explain how routing and forwarding is implemented on the Internet, including the design and implementation of network-layer protocols Describe and explain different link-layer technologies and how they work In addition, you are expected to build a basic understanding of three example topics: Network security: Exemplify how different types of security services can be implemented in different layers with the help of different standards Wireless and mobile networks: Analyze and exemplify some of the unique challenges as we are moving towards increasingly mobile users Multimedia networking: Explain and discuss the fundamentals of how multimedia services are provided over the Internet
specialisations: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security
curriculum_notes: Students with TDTS04 or TDTS11 in their bachelor's degree choose another course from the specialization to meet the requirements.

detail.title: Computer Networks, 6 credits
detail.name: Computer Networks
detail.description: After the course, you are expected to be able to: Explain, describe, and analyze a typical network architecture, including the importance of network layers and encapsulation Explain the different basic types of protocols, communication channels, and network types Design, implement, verify, and test your own protocols Explain fundamental performance tradeoffs, including showing an understanding of where delays can occur in a network, what different types of delay that exist, the impact of packet losses and jitter on various protocols Overall, you should have an applied understanding of the network architecture and the protocols associated with the different layers, as well as how they are implemented: Describe and analyze the most common application architectures in the Internet, how the most important application-layer protocols work, the service they provide, as well as have the ability to design and implement their own application-layer protocols Analyze and explain important design considerations at the transport layer, including hands-on knowledge of how flow control and congestion control works, and how reliable data transfer is implemented Motivate and explain how routing and forwarding is implemented on the Internet, including the design and implementation of network-layer protocols Describe and explain different link-layer technologies and how they work In addition, you are expected to build a basic understanding of three example topics: Network security: Exemplify how different types of security services can be implemented in different layers with the help of different standards Wireless and mobile networks: Analyze and exemplify some of the unique challenges as we are moving towards increasingly mobile users Multimedia networking: Explain and discuss the fundamentals of how multimedia services are provided over the Internet
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 44 h Recommended self-study hours: 116 h
detail.prerequisites: Knowledge of C or C++ are required in order to be able to do the laborations in the course. It is an advantage if the student also has knowledge corresponding to Concurrent Programming and Operating Systems, especially know how to explain the resource conflicts that can occur in a computer program and how to solve them. This knowledge can, however, be acquired while doing the laborations in the course. The student is also assumed to know how to construct and test programs in a Unix/Solaris environment.

detail.specific_information: Can not be included in degree together with TDDD93, TDDE35, TDTS04 or TDTS11.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After the course, you are expected to be able to: Explain, describe, and analyze a typical network architecture, including the importance of network layers and encapsulation Explain the different basic types of protocols, communication channels, and network types Design, implement, verify, and test your own protocols Explain fundamental performance tradeoffs, including showing an understanding of where delays can occur in a network, what different types of delay that exist, the impact of packet losses and jitter on various protocols Overall, you should have an applied understanding of the network architecture and the protocols associated with the different layers, as well as how they are implemented: Describe and analyze the most common application architectures in the Internet, how the most important application-layer protocols work, the service they provide, as well as have the ability to design and implement their own application-layer protocols Analyze and explain important design considerations at the transport layer, including hands-on knowledge of how flow control and congestion control works, and how reliable data transfer is implemented Motivate and explain how routing and forwarding is implemented on the Internet, including the design and implementation of network-layer protocols Describe and explain different link-layer technologies and how they work In addition, you are expected to build a basic understanding of three example topics: Network security: Exemplify how different types of security services can be implemented in different layers with the help of different standards Wireless and mobile networks: Analyze and exemplify some of the unique challenges as we are moving towards increasingly mobile users Multimedia networking: Explain and discuss the fundamentals of how multimedia services are provided over the Internet
detail.course_content: Protocol terminology, language, and specification. The protocol layering concept. Reference models for network architectures. Application areas for computer networks and examples of commercial network services. Network types and components (router, switch, repeater, hub). Communicaton modes and channels. Access network technology. Different types of MAC protocols. The collision domain concept. The sliding window protocol. Error detection. Local area networks (IEEE 802.3). Wireless networks (Bluetooth, WiFi and WiMax). Extending LANs. Internet and standardisation. The TCP/IP protocol family. Distance vector and link state routing. ICMP. ARP. NAT. Naming, addressing, and routing on the Internet. TCP timers, flow control, and congestion control. TCP reliable delivery. Three-way handshake. IPv6. Mobile IP. QoS network parameters and frameworks. Network performance issues. Internet applications (DNS, e-mail, ftp, the web, filesharing, IP telephony, and SNMP). IP telephony. Network security applications (IPsec, SSL/TLS, PGP). Key management. WPA2. P2P networks. Bittorrent, the DHT data structure and Skype. Internet history. Internet design principles. LAN background. Development trends. SDN.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures and laborations.
detail.examination: UPG1 Voluntary assignment 0 credits U, G LAB1 Laboratory work 3 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.
detail.other_information: Supplementary courses: Advanced Networking, Individual projects

offerings.name: Computer Networks
offerings.description: After the course, you are expected to be able to: Explain, describe, and analyze a typical network architecture, including the importance of network layers and encapsulation Explain the different basic types of protocols, communication channels, and network types Design, implement, verify, and test your own protocols Explain fundamental performance tradeoffs, including showing an understanding of where delays can occur in a network, what different types of delay that exist, the impact of packet losses and jitter on various protocols Overall, you should have an applied understanding of the network architecture and the protocols associated with the different layers, as well as how they are implemented: Describe and analyze the most common application architectures in the Internet, how the most important application-layer protocols work, the service they provide, as well as have the ability to design and implement their own application-layer protocols Analyze and explain important design considerations at the transport layer, including hands-on knowledge of how flow control and congestion control works, and how reliable data transfer is implemented Motivate and explain how routing and forwarding is implemented on the Internet, including the design and implementation of network-layer protocols Describe and explain different link-layer technologies and how they work In addition, you are expected to build a basic understanding of three example topics: Network security: Exemplify how different types of security services can be implemented in different layers with the help of different standards Wireless and mobile networks: Analyze and exemplify some of the unique challenges as we are moving towards increasingly mobile users Multimedia networking: Explain and discuss the fundamentals of how multimedia services are provided over the Internet

courses.name: System Design and Methodology
courses.description: The aim of the course is to address the particular problems concerning the design of complex embedded systems. Modern design methodologies are presented with an emphasis on early design phases, such as modeling, verification and system-level synthesis, not covered by traditional methods. After completing the course, the students should be able to: Apply modern system-level methods and tools for the design of real-time embedded systems. Understand and use modern modeling, verification and simulation tools in the context of system-level design. Apply various modeling approaches, with a deep understanding of the practical and theoretical implications. Analyze the particular features of the application and select the most appropriate modeling approach. Evaluate the implications of system level design decisions, regarding system architecture, task scheduling and mapping, on the final system performance, cost, and power consumption. Understand the complex interactions between hardware architecture and software implementation. Formally verify a system model. Perform design space exploration using a system-level simulation environment.

detail.course_content: Embedded systems and their design, Design flow, Specification and modeling of embedded systems, Simulation and estimation, Architectures for embedded systems,Mapping and scheduling, Power related issues at the system level.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of a series of lectures, lessons and laboratory exercises, as well as the elaboration and presentation of a report.
detail.examination: DIT1 Digital examination 2.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB2 Laboratory Work 3.5 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Advanced Computer Architecture
courses.description: After the course, students should be able to * understand how modern computer systems work internally * describe advanced concepts and principles of computer architecture and organization * describe methods to improve the performance of current microprocessors and parallel systems * analyze a computer to extract its basic architectural features * describe how computer components can be used to efficiently accomplish computation tasks

detail.title: Advanced Computer Architecture, 6 credits
detail.name: Advanced Computer Architecture
detail.description: After the course, students should be able to * understand how modern computer systems work internally * describe advanced concepts and principles of computer architecture and organization * describe methods to improve the performance of current microprocessors and parallel systems * analyze a computer to extract its basic architectural features * describe how computer components can be used to efficiently accomplish computation tasks
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Electrical Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 48 h Recommended self-study hours: 112 h
detail.prerequisites: Computer Hardware and Architecture
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master's Programme in Electronics Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Cybersecurity
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After the course, students should be able to * understand how modern computer systems work internally * describe advanced concepts and principles of computer architecture and organization * describe methods to improve the performance of current microprocessors and parallel systems * analyze a computer to extract its basic architectural features * describe how computer components can be used to efficiently accomplish computation tasks
detail.course_content: Instruction set, memory management and hierarchy, input/output and buses, pipelining techniques, branch prediction, RISC architectures, VLIW architectures and specific compiling techniques, superscalar architectures, out of order execution, parallel architectures and multiprocessors.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is organized as a set of lectures and laboratory exercises.
detail.examination: LAB1 Laboratory work 2 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Advanced Networking
courses.description: Using a combination of theory and exploration of real data, participants will gain a deeper understanding of modern computer networks, applications, and network services. The course complements the basic computer network courses both in depth and in breadth and will focus on current "hot topics" within the computer network domain. Participants successfully completing the course will have experience and be able to: explain, in detail, a wide range of technologies used to build data networks; explain using concrete examples fundamental network design principles and scalability tradeoffs; predict and explain how different networking technologies at the same or different layers interact and affect each other in a large-scale system; critically evaluate network technologies with respect to system requirements, based on information from current research and technical documentation; design and perform targeted experiments to critically evaluate network technologies, applications, and services; apply basic system models and analysis methods to analyze distributed systems and networks; plan and conduct an extensive study of an identified problem within a selected area of technology, including integrating knowledge from multiple sources such as current research, technical documentation and experiments from real data sources (in some cases collected by the students themselves); generalize and synthesize information from multiple sources (and types of sources) in the computer network area to form original and well-motivated conclusions; and based on an in-depth study, present and explain (both written and orally) findings within a selected area of technology, to an audience with similar general knowledge of computer networks.
specialisations: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

detail.title: Advanced Networking, 6 credits
detail.name: Advanced Networking
detail.description: Using a combination of theory and exploration of real data, participants will gain a deeper understanding of modern computer networks, applications, and network services. The course complements the basic computer network courses both in depth and in breadth and will focus on current "hot topics" within the computer network domain. Participants successfully completing the course will have experience and be able to: explain, in detail, a wide range of technologies used to build data networks; explain using concrete examples fundamental network design principles and scalability tradeoffs; predict and explain how different networking technologies at the same or different layers interact and affect each other in a large-scale system; critically evaluate network technologies with respect to system requirements, based on information from current research and technical documentation; design and perform targeted experiments to critically evaluate network technologies, applications, and services; apply basic system models and analysis methods to analyze distributed systems and networks; plan and conduct an extensive study of an identified problem within a selected area of technology, including integrating knowledge from multiple sources such as current research, technical documentation and experiments from real data sources (in some cases collected by the students themselves); generalize and synthesize information from multiple sources (and types of sources) in the computer network area to form original and well-motivated conclusions; and based on an in-depth study, present and explain (both written and orally) findings within a selected area of technology, to an audience with similar general knowledge of computer networks.
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 30 h Recommended self-study hours: 130 h
detail.prerequisites: Basic computer networks course. (For LiU students, this normally corresponds to having taken TDTS06, TDDE35/TDDD93, TDTS11, eller TDTS04.)
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: Using a combination of theory and exploration of real data, participants will gain a deeper understanding of modern computer networks, applications, and network services. The course complements the basic computer network courses both in depth and in breadth and will focus on current "hot topics" within the computer network domain. Participants successfully completing the course will have experience and be able to: explain, in detail, a wide range of technologies used to build data networks; explain using concrete examples fundamental network design principles and scalability tradeoffs; predict and explain how different networking technologies at the same or different layers interact and affect each other in a large-scale system; critically evaluate network technologies with respect to system requirements, based on information from current research and technical documentation; design and perform targeted experiments to critically evaluate network technologies, applications, and services; apply basic system models and analysis methods to analyze distributed systems and networks; plan and conduct an extensive study of an identified problem within a selected area of technology, including integrating knowledge from multiple sources such as current research, technical documentation and experiments from real data sources (in some cases collected by the students themselves); generalize and synthesize information from multiple sources (and types of sources) in the computer network area to form original and well-motivated conclusions; and based on an in-depth study, present and explain (both written and orally) findings within a selected area of technology, to an audience with similar general knowledge of computer networks.
detail.course_content: The precise contents of the course vary slightly from year to year, to keep up with developments in the area, and to focus on currently "hot topics" (e.g., SDN, cloud, VR over network, ML-based traffic classification/detection ...). Recurring topics include: Fundamental properties of computer networks (e.g., Power laws, rich-gets-richer); Scalable systems and designs (e.g., hierarchical vs. flat designs; layered designs); Protocol interactions (e.g., between common protocols such as HTTP, TCP, IP, Ethernet, as well as more application/domain specific protocols), Measurement, modeling and analysis methods using real network data; Important modern computer architectures (e.g., cloud services such as EC2, CDNs, the Internet routing architecture itself, smart grids, and social networks).
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of both theory (lectures, seminars, and paper discussions) and practical hands-on training and exploration (project). The underlying theme of the course is to use real data and experiments to understand network infrastructures and their services. The course has a written final exam. The project should result in a written report, should be presented in a seminar during which the students will act as both presenters and opponents (evaluating and providing each other with feedback, such as to improve the reports and projects). The course runs over the entire spring semester.
detail.examination: PRA1 Project assignment with oral presentation 3.5 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination 2.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Active participation in the seminars is compulsory. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.
detail.other_information: Supplementary courses: Thesis or individual project.

courses.name: Advanced Global Illumination and Rendering
courses.description: The aim of this course is to provide the student with in-depth knowledge of global illumination algorithms. After this course, the student should be able to: explain the raytracing concept for the illumination of scenes involving mirrors and transparent surfaces. understand the idea of Monte-Carlo integration and how it is used to illuminate diffuse surfaces in Monte-Carlo raytracing. explain what photon mapping is and how it can be used to resolve caustics.
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)

detail.title: Advanced Global Illumination and Rendering, 6 credits
detail.name: Advanced Global Illumination and Rendering
detail.description: The aim of this course is to provide the student with in-depth knowledge of global illumination algorithms. After this course, the student should be able to: explain the raytracing concept for the illumination of scenes involving mirrors and transparent surfaces. understand the idea of Monte-Carlo integration and how it is used to illuminate diffuse surfaces in Monte-Carlo raytracing. explain what photon mapping is and how it can be used to resolve caustics.
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science, Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 36 h Recommended self-study hours: 124 h
detail.prerequisites: Computer graphics, 3D Computer Graphics and Animation
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The aim of this course is to provide the student with in-depth knowledge of global illumination algorithms. After this course, the student should be able to: explain the raytracing concept for the illumination of scenes involving mirrors and transparent surfaces. understand the idea of Monte-Carlo integration and how it is used to illuminate diffuse surfaces in Monte-Carlo raytracing. explain what photon mapping is and how it can be used to resolve caustics.
detail.course_content: The course will include the following topic areas: optics and light, ray tracing algorithms, Monte Carlo sampling and integration,general strategy for solving the rendering equation, stochastic path tracing, photon mapping, caustics, perception and display.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: Background lectures, followed by student research paper presentations, followed by project work.
detail.examination: UPG1 Project work with oral and written presentation 6 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Evaluation of the course will be in the form of an assessment of an oral presentation by the students in the middle of the period. A software project will also form a part of the course and this project, together with the project report, will be graded. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

offerings.description: The aim of this course is to provide the student with in-depth knowledge of global illumination algorithms. After this course, the student should be able to: explain the raytracing concept for the illumination of scenes involving mirrors and transparent surfaces. understand the idea of Monte-Carlo integration and how it is used to illuminate diffuse surfaces in Monte-Carlo raytracing. explain what photon mapping is and how it can be used to resolve caustics.

detail.title: 3D Computer Graphics, 6 credits
detail.name: 3D Computer Graphics
detail.description: This course provides an overview of concepts and techniques commonly employed in 3D computer graphics. After this course, students should be able to name, explain, and apply basic concepts used in 3D computer graphics select and motivate methods and techniques to solve common problems in the field use 3D computer graphics software for the modeling, animation, and rendering of simple scenes
detail.main_field_of_study: Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 52 h Recommended self-study hours: 108 h
detail.prerequisites: Computer Graphics
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: This course provides an overview of concepts and techniques commonly employed in 3D computer graphics. After this course, students should be able to name, explain, and apply basic concepts used in 3D computer graphics select and motivate methods and techniques to solve common problems in the field use 3D computer graphics software for the modeling, animation, and rendering of simple scenes
detail.course_content: 3D graphics: scene graphs, transformations, data structures real-time graphics: rendering pipeline, graphics hardware, shading modeling: polygon meshes, parametric curves and surfaces materials: light-object interactions, texturing global illumination: ray tracing, radiosity animation using keyframes, inverse kinematics, physics-based simulations
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: Lectures, lab exercises, and an extensive project assignment. Lectures are based on fundamental concepts, while the labs and project presents an opportunity to put these concepts into practice. The course is given over the entire spring semester and combines theory and practice.
detail.examination: UPG1 Group project assignment 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laboratory course 1.5 credits U, G TEN1 Written exam 1.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 The final grade is a mean value of the grades for TEN1 and UPG1, where both grades are given equal weight and any rounding is done upwards. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Scientific Visualization
courses.description: The goal for this course is to provide the student with deep insights into methods for visualization of scientific data from experiments and simulations. The applicability of the various methods is shown through practical programming exercises. Upon completion of the course the student should be able to: For a given data set choose an appropriate visualization method. Design and implement a visualization tool using the chosen. method and available software toolkits. Read and present the content in scientific papers in the field.
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)

detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science, Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 48 h Recommended self-study hours: 112 h
detail.prerequisites: Computer Graphics, Physical modeling
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Biomedical Engineering Master's Programme in Biomedical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Mathematics
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The goal for this course is to provide the student with deep insights into methods for visualization of scientific data from experiments and simulations. The applicability of the various methods is shown through practical programming exercises. Upon completion of the course the student should be able to: For a given data set choose an appropriate visualization method. Design and implement a visualization tool using the chosen. method and available software toolkits. Read and present the content in scientific papers in the field.
detail.course_content: Introduction to visualization: visualization as a research field, applications, tasks Visualization pipeline Data representation and interpolation: Basic data types: Scalar, vector and tensor data Structured and unstructured data Basic visualization algorithms for scalar fields, e.g. color mapping, contour lines and surfaces for vector fields, e.g. flow lines and surfaces and time animation of these for tensor fields, e.g. glyphs, tensor lines Overview of techniques for volume rendering Introduction to concepts for more advanced visualizations data analysis data exploration feature extraction topological methods Examples of some application specific visualization techniques
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is composed of lectures and laboratory assignments. Scientific papers will also be included as self-study material.
detail.examination: MUN1 Oral examination 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laboratory work 3 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Modelling and Animation
courses.description: The aim of the course is to give the students knowledge of current and advanced methods in computer graphics for modeling and animation of virtual objects. After completing the course the student shall be able to: understand and evaluate different mathematical representations of surfaces in 3D describe and implement alternative data structures and decimation methods for polygon meshes describe and implement 3D models using subdivision surfaces and implicitly defined surfaces understand the principles and methods of modelling using level-sets describe and implement the principles and methods used in simulation and animation of fluids Course content Representations of surfaces in 3D. Triangular meshes in modeling. Mesh decimation. Implicit modeling. The level-set method in computer graphics and modeling. Splines and subdivision surfaces. Physics based simulation and animation of fluids. Teaching and working methods The course starts with a series of lectures, providing an overview and presenting the theory in the field. The lecture material is accompanied by a set of laboratory exercises to demonstrate the theory in action. At the end of the course a written exam is given. To complete the course, students must have passed the lab exercises and the written exam. Grades are based both on the lab exercises and the written exam. Examination LAB4 Laboratory work 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 TEN3 Written examination 2 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course. Grades Four-grade scale, LiU, U, 3, 4, 5 Other information About teaching and examination language The teaching language is presented in the Overview tab for each course. The examination language relates to the teaching language as follows: If teaching language is “Swedish”, the course as a whole could be given in Swedish, or partly in English. Examination language is Swedish, but parts of the examination can be in English. If teaching language is “English”, the course as a whole is taught in English. Examination language is English. If teaching language is “Swedish/English”, the course as a whole will be taught in English if students without prior knowledge of the Swedish language participate. Examination language is Swedish or English depending on teaching language. Other The course is conducted in such a way that there are equal opportunities with regard to sex, transgender identity or expression, ethnicity, religion or other belief, disability, sexual orientation and age. The planning and implementation of a course should correspond to the course syllabus. The course evaluation should therefore be conducted with the course syllabus as a starting point. The course is campus-based at the location specified for the course, unless otherwise stated under “Teaching and working methods”. Please note, in a campus-based course occasional remote sessions could be included. courses.name: Procedural Methods for Images
courses.description: In order to complete the course, the student must be able to: describe images and geometries as tools in modern computer graphics. describe classic and modern methods for procedural generation of images, especially textures that aim to mimic complicated and visually detailed phenomena in nature. be able to use general and special purpose programming languages to generate images and certain types of geometry be able to describe and implement a selection of classic methods for procedural generation of patterns. carry out a project that solves a real problem that is formulated in terms of the desired visual result, where the student must be able to choose, motivate, implement, describe and to some extent evaluate their own solution based on existing, published methods.
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)

detail.title: Procedural Methods for Images, 6 credits
detail.name: Procedural Methods for Images
detail.description: In order to complete the course, the student must be able to: describe images and geometries as tools in modern computer graphics. describe classic and modern methods for procedural generation of images, especially textures that aim to mimic complicated and visually detailed phenomena in nature. be able to use general and special purpose programming languages to generate images and certain types of geometry be able to describe and implement a selection of classic methods for procedural generation of patterns. carry out a project that solves a real problem that is formulated in terms of the desired visual result, where the student must be able to choose, motivate, implement, describe and to some extent evaluate their own solution based on existing, published methods.
detail.main_field_of_study: Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 44 h Recommended self-study hours: 116 h
detail.prerequisites: Calculus, multivariate calculus, linear algebra. Basic computer graphics. Technical aspects of 3D modeling and animation. Programming plus Object Oriented Programming, Data Structures and Algorithms. Knowledge in image processing and image analysis and deep learning is desirable and useful as a prerequisite, but not required.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: In order to complete the course, the student must be able to: describe images and geometries as tools in modern computer graphics. describe classic and modern methods for procedural generation of images, especially textures that aim to mimic complicated and visually detailed phenomena in nature. be able to use general and special purpose programming languages to generate images and certain types of geometry be able to describe and implement a selection of classic methods for procedural generation of patterns. carry out a project that solves a real problem that is formulated in terms of the desired visual result, where the student must be able to choose, motivate, implement, describe and to some extent evaluate their own solution based on existing, published methods.
detail.course_content: The principle behind procedural images, their use, advantages and disadvantages of texturing. Textures in 2, 3 and 4 dimensions. Regular and irregular patterns. Random number generation. Noise functions, especially "Perlin noise" and its relatives (lattice noises). Multifractal synthesis and simulation of turbulence. Fractals. Splines. Fractal Brownian Motion. Direct Fourier synthesis. Programming in general programming languages for procedural images. Shading languages. Functions for texture synthesis and geometry synthesis in modern graphics hardware. Introduction to deep learning methods.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course includes lectures, programming-oriented laboratory work and a project assignment. The project assignment is chosen relatively freely in consultation with the examiner. It is performed individually or in groups depending on the number of course participants and the course participant's preference. This is done independently with some guidance, and is reported in writing and orally for assessment.
detail.examination: LAB1 Laboratory work 3 credits U, G UPG1 Project 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

offerings.description: In order to complete the course, the student must be able to: describe images and geometries as tools in modern computer graphics. describe classic and modern methods for procedural generation of images, especially textures that aim to mimic complicated and visually detailed phenomena in nature. be able to use general and special purpose programming languages to generate images and certain types of geometry be able to describe and implement a selection of classic methods for procedural generation of patterns. carry out a project that solves a real problem that is formulated in terms of the desired visual result, where the student must be able to choose, motivate, implement, describe and to some extent evaluate their own solution based on existing, published methods.

courses.name: Media Production for Immersive Environments
courses.description: After completion of the course the student will: be able to use tools for the production and handling of immersive images, video and sound as well as program immersive real time graphics, be able to individually or in group plan, produce and present immersive media productions where the immersive techniques are effectively utilized to convey a message, and be able to critically as well as professionally analyze and reflect over existing technologies and practices for immersive production.
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)
curriculum_notes: *The course is divided into several semesters and/or periods

detail.title: Media Production for Immersive Environments, 6 credits
detail.name: Media Production for Immersive Environments
detail.description: After completion of the course the student will: be able to use tools for the production and handling of immersive images, video and sound as well as program immersive real time graphics, be able to individually or in group plan, produce and present immersive media productions where the immersive techniques are effectively utilized to convey a message, and be able to critically as well as professionally analyze and reflect over existing technologies and practices for immersive production.
detail.main_field_of_study: Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 42 h Recommended self-study hours: 118 h
detail.prerequisites: Programming skills in C++. Linear Algebra. Experience of 3D computer graphics. Beneficial but not a requirement: basic sound physics and video production.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After completion of the course the student will: be able to use tools for the production and handling of immersive images, video and sound as well as program immersive real time graphics, be able to individually or in group plan, produce and present immersive media productions where the immersive techniques are effectively utilized to convey a message, and be able to critically as well as professionally analyze and reflect over existing technologies and practices for immersive production.
detail.course_content: In this course we study and explore the technical and artistic considerations in media production for immersive environments such as head mounted displays, domes, VR cubes and curved screen environments. Such environments offer great opportunities for the creation of exciting and unusual media forms but present special challenges in the design of media and in the contents preparation. The course is based upon practical use of technically advanced media technology, such as combinations of (stereo) video, computer graphics (interactive and pre rendered), immersive audio and other media suitable for the presentation environment.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: A couple of lectures are given to present principles and techniques for immersive media and production, and to suggest directions and approaches to media production. A couple of laboratory exercises are given to familiarize the studient with the equipment and some common techniques for contents production. The main focus of the course is, however, on the individual learning and work in the project.
detail.examination: PRA2 Project 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB2 Laboratory Work 1.5 credits U, G UPG2 Assignment 0.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Examination is based on participation in the various stages of the production process and the students’ individual contributions, as well as an assessment of the finished project. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Advanced Visual Data Analysis
courses.description: After completing the course, the student should be able to: Examine new, complex data sets and identify relevant features which might be extracted. Select and apply algorithmic methods for analysis of large complex data sets to determine valuable results. Select and apply well-established visualization methods to display extracted relevant information from such data sets. Describe and reflect on visualization techniques (regarding interaction and visual representation) and example tools for special data sets and applications domains based on theory and practice discussed in the course. Plan and implement a data analysis approach combining interactive visualization techniques with algorithmic methods in a given context. Make well-grounded design choices in the context of various tasks and data constraints and be able to motivate and justify these choices.
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)

detail.title: Advanced Visual Data Analysis, 6 credits
detail.name: Advanced Visual Data Analysis
detail.description: After completing the course, the student should be able to: Examine new, complex data sets and identify relevant features which might be extracted. Select and apply algorithmic methods for analysis of large complex data sets to determine valuable results. Select and apply well-established visualization methods to display extracted relevant information from such data sets. Describe and reflect on visualization techniques (regarding interaction and visual representation) and example tools for special data sets and applications domains based on theory and practice discussed in the course. Plan and implement a data analysis approach combining interactive visualization techniques with algorithmic methods in a given context. Make well-grounded design choices in the context of various tasks and data constraints and be able to motivate and justify these choices.
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science, Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 48 h Recommended self-study hours: 112 h
detail.prerequisites: Skills in mathematics, programming and web programming. Knowledge in information visualization via the course in Information Visualization or equivalent. Knowledge in and/or interest for data mining/machine learning is advantageous but not a requirement.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After completing the course, the student should be able to: Examine new, complex data sets and identify relevant features which might be extracted. Select and apply algorithmic methods for analysis of large complex data sets to determine valuable results. Select and apply well-established visualization methods to display extracted relevant information from such data sets. Describe and reflect on visualization techniques (regarding interaction and visual representation) and example tools for special data sets and applications domains based on theory and practice discussed in the course. Plan and implement a data analysis approach combining interactive visualization techniques with algorithmic methods in a given context. Make well-grounded design choices in the context of various tasks and data constraints and be able to motivate and justify these choices.
detail.course_content: This course builds upon the course Information Visualization, with a focus on the data modelling, mining and analysis techniques with are the foundation of modern visual data analysis methodology. Such methods are becoming very important as the scale of data available for analysis expands, leading to the so-called 'big data' problem affecting business, healthcare, government, science and industry. Topics covered: Introduction to the field of visual data analysis and relevant concepts, such as visual analytics (VA), knowledge generation model for VA, human-in-the-loop and human-centered analysis. Data aspects that affect analysis. Visualization techniques for spatial, temporal, tree / network, text data and combinations of these. Interaction concept that promotes people-centered analysis. Overview of techniques for automatic analysis support, such as techniques for abstraction, comparison, pattern detection, dimension reduction, text mining.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is composed of lectures, laboratory assignments, seminar sessions and a substantial project work.
detail.examination: LAB2 Laboratory work 1 credits U, G PRA1 Project assignment 5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Scientific Method
courses.description: After the course, students shall be able to: Evaluate texts with respect to scientific and engineering standards. Select and evaluate relevant scientific and engineering methods in the topic area for each student. Formulate a scientific text using an academic standard. Formulate and criticize a plan for a scientific study. Critically evaluate scientific works. Seek information about and evaluate references in their own topic area. Summarize scientific results in their topic area. Assess and manage ethical issues and societal aspects of science and engineering in their topic area.
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)
curriculum_notes: For the Master's program Computer Science, either TDDD89 or TNM107 must be included in the degree. Select TNM107 for the “Visualization and Computer Graphics” specialization. The course is replaced by an optional course for students with TDIU14 or TDP026 in Bachelor's degree.

detail.title: Scientific Method, 6 credits
detail.name: Scientific Method
detail.description: After the course, students shall be able to: Evaluate texts with respect to scientific and engineering standards. Select and evaluate relevant scientific and engineering methods in the topic area for each student. Formulate a scientific text using an academic standard. Formulate and criticize a plan for a scientific study. Critically evaluate scientific works. Seek information about and evaluate references in their own topic area. Summarize scientific results in their topic area. Assess and manage ethical issues and societal aspects of science and engineering in their topic area.
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 36 h Recommended self-study hours: 124 h Possible deviation from course syllabus/programme syllabus
detail.prerequisites: Prerequisites: (valid for students admitted to programmes within which the course is offered) 30hp on advanced level in main subject area. Experience in reading scientific peer-reviewed papers in main subject area.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.specific_information: The course is replaced by an elective course for students with TDIU14 or TDP026 in bachelor’s degree. The course can not be included in degree together with TDDD89.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After the course, students shall be able to: Evaluate texts with respect to scientific and engineering standards. Select and evaluate relevant scientific and engineering methods in the topic area for each student. Formulate a scientific text using an academic standard. Formulate and criticize a plan for a scientific study. Critically evaluate scientific works. Seek information about and evaluate references in their own topic area. Summarize scientific results in their topic area. Assess and manage ethical issues and societal aspects of science and engineering in their topic area.
detail.course_content: Scientific methods: their purpose, quantitative methods, qualitative methods. Engineering and science Scientific writing Literature search and summary Critical analysis of scientific work: reliability and validity Reference management: specificity and completeness. Ethics in research Societal aspects of research and technical development.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is conducted as a series of lectures, seminars and hand-in assignments. The course is performed as a preparatory study before a master's thesis project, including formulating research questions, literature review and survey of theoretical reference material, and an initial description of a research method. In the course, scientific aspects of thesis projects are analyzed during seminars. During the course, scientific methods are presented in the main topic areas for students taking the course.
detail.examination: UPG2 Seminars 4 credits U, G UPG1 Hand-in assignments 2 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.
detail.grades: Two grade scale, older version, U, G

Describe validation and evaluation methods for information visualization tools and approaches. detail.title: Information Visualization, 6 credits
detail.name: Information Visualization
detail.description: After completing the course, the student shall be able to: Classify typical user tasks for visualizations in context of the domain situation. Define and explain the visualization techniques (regarding interaction and visual representation) and example tools discussed in the course. Describe validation and evaluation methods for information visualization tools and approaches. Represent data by expressive and effective visualizations using state­of­the­ art methods, software, and tools. Critically reflect on visualization and interaction approaches in the light of current theories and research. Make well­grounded design choices in the context of various tasks and data constraints.
detail.main_field_of_study: Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science, Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 51 h Recommended self-study hours: 109 h
detail.prerequisites: Programming. The course “Practical Data Visualization and Virtual Reality” as well as skills in visual perception, computer graphics and web programming are beneficial but not required.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.specific_information: The course can not be included in degree together with TNM048.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After completing the course, the student shall be able to: Classify typical user tasks for visualizations in context of the domain situation. Define and explain the visualization techniques (regarding interaction and visual representation) and example tools discussed in the course. Describe validation and evaluation methods for information visualization tools and approaches. Represent data by expressive and effective visualizations using state­of­the­ art methods, software, and tools. Critically reflect on visualization and interaction approaches in the light of current theories and research. Make well­grounded design choices in the context of various tasks and data constraints.
detail.course_content: Information visualization centers on abstract information that does not have a physical or geometric correspondence to the real world. Examples of such abstract data are symbolic, tabular, networked, hierarchical, or textual information sources. The course provides an overview of the most common information visualization techniques and their application. The following topics are covered: Definition of the field of information visualization and relationships to related fields such as human­-computer interaction or scientific visualization Data and visualization foundations and processing Data and task abstractions Interaction concepts and techniques (e.g., dynamic queries, zoom and pan, or focus and context) Visualization techniques for 1D, 2D, 3D, and multidimensional data Visualization techniques and drawing conventions for trees/hierarchies, general network data (graphs), and multivariate/dynamic networks. Validating and evaluating visualizations Overview of current systems and toolkits for information visualization
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is composed of lectures, theoretical and practical (programming) assignments, and seminar sessions in which the handed-in assignment reports are presented and discussed. Scientific papers will also be included as self-study material.
detail.examination: UPG1 Assignments with oral and written presentations 3 credits U, G MUN1 Oral examination 3 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

detail.course_content: Intelligent methods of exploring virtual worlds Artificial Life for Virtual Worlds AI techniques to build virtual worlds Behavioral animation Machine learning; reinforcement learning, decision trees Deep learning Natural language processing Virtual assistants - Chatbots Generative AI Genetic Algorithms Swarm Intelligence Emotion-based decision making
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The lectures are devoted to a theoretical review of the course content. Students are also required to actively attend the other students’ oral presentations. The project consist of development, presentation and a written report. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: eXtended Reality (XR) - Principles and Programming
courses.description: The aim of the course is for the student to gain insights in XR, VR, AR and other related principles, how they are used and how they can be implemented and utillized. They should also learn to analyze needs and challenges and learn how to apply theories and principles to realize effective immersive user interfaces and interaction. After finishing this course the student should be able to present the principles of XR with respect to human factors such as perception, social aspects and gender visual and multisensory display systems, tracking and other interaction equipment egocentric interaction and haptics audio and audio interaction implement interactive, immersive applications
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)

detail.title: eXtended Reality (XR) - Principles and Programming, 6 credits
detail.name: eXtended Reality (XR) - Principles and Programming
detail.description: The aim of the course is for the student to gain insights in XR, VR, AR and other related principles, how they are used and how they can be implemented and utillized. They should also learn to analyze needs and challenges and learn how to apply theories and principles to realize effective immersive user interfaces and interaction. After finishing this course the student should be able to present the principles of XR with respect to human factors such as perception, social aspects and gender visual and multisensory display systems, tracking and other interaction equipment egocentric interaction and haptics audio and audio interaction implement interactive, immersive applications
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Media Technology and Engineering
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 0 h Recommended self-study hours: 160 h
detail.prerequisites: 3D Computer Graphics, Linear Algebra, Programminig in C++
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Media Technology and Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Biomedical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.specific_information: The course can not be included in degree together with TNM086.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The aim of the course is for the student to gain insights in XR, VR, AR and other related principles, how they are used and how they can be implemented and utillized. They should also learn to analyze needs and challenges and learn how to apply theories and principles to realize effective immersive user interfaces and interaction. After finishing this course the student should be able to present the principles of XR with respect to human factors such as perception, social aspects and gender visual and multisensory display systems, tracking and other interaction equipment egocentric interaction and haptics audio and audio interaction implement interactive, immersive applications
detail.course_content: The course covers a wide range of aspects associated with eXtended Reality (XR), a collective term for immersive applications with virtual content, such as virtual reality (VR), augmented reality (AR), mixed reality (MR) and augmented virtuality. We cover display systems and their principles, from desktop to large-scale VR theaters and domes, principles for effective implementation, methods for interaction and how sound and touch are used to create a convincing and useful immersive environment for a range of different applications.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course is given at Campus Norrköping, but can to an extent be taken remotely, if necessary. Attendance is required for laboration with VR equipment and examination with VR equipment. Theories are covered through lectures supplemented with scientific publications for further in-depth studies. Substantial laboratory work is also included, where theory is converted into practice. Students taking the course are given access to the VR laboratory and its equipment.
detail.examination: MUN1 Oral examination 2 credits U, 3, 4, 5 LAB1 Laborations 4 credits U, G Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

detail.course_content: Determined individually for each student in consultation with the examiner and supervisor. The project must be carried out in the main field of study.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of an independent degree project. A supervisor and an examiner are appointed for each student or group of students. The degree project is the final step before graduation.
detail.examination: AUSK Attendance at three thesis presentations 0.5 credits D OPPO Opposition 1.5 credits U, G UPG1 Planning report, midway assessment, written report, oral presentation and reflection document 28 credits U, G Only degree projects at a level and extent equal to that of your personal degree project can be selected for opposition. The student must oppose at least one degree project. Attendance at thesis presentations may be done starting the first semester of the master’s programme and is recorded, until registration of the master’s thesis can be done, in the course code TEXMAS. Thesis presentation attendance can be done at degree projects at a level equal to or higher than your personal degree project. The written report should consist of a manuscript ready for publication together with an individual document regarding the completed degree project. The course is graded Pass/Fail. Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.
detail.grades: Two grade scale, older version, U, G

courses.name: Image and Audio Compression
courses.description: The course should give knowledge about methods used for data compression and how these methods are applied to audio, image and video signals. After taking the course, the student is expected to be able to Make random models for sources Analyze random source models Understand source coding methods such as huffman coding and arithmetic coding. Understand quantization Understand linear predictive coding Understand transform coding. Understand subband coding. Calculate coding performance for different coding methods, given random models for the sources Know how different coding methods are used for compression of still images, video and audio data. Know what methods are used in different compression standards (GIF, PNG, JPEG, MPEG, mp3, AAC, speech kodning).
specialisations: Visualization and Computer Graphics (semester 2, 3 at Campus Norrköping)

courses.name: Information Networks
courses.description: After completing the course, the student should be able to: describe the slotted multiple-access model, Poisson packet arrival model, and multiple-access protocols to a shared medium. give an account of the properties of mechanisms in protocols for multiple access to a shared medium. formulate assumptions to model and analyze information networks and discuss the validity of these assumptions. perform typical calculations to implement the various algorithms covered in the course.
specialisations: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security

detail.title: Information Networks, 6 credits
detail.name: Information Networks
detail.description: After completing the course, the student should be able to: describe the slotted multiple-access model, Poisson packet arrival model, and multiple-access protocols to a shared medium. give an account of the properties of mechanisms in protocols for multiple access to a shared medium. formulate assumptions to model and analyze information networks and discuss the validity of these assumptions. perform typical calculations to implement the various algorithms covered in the course.
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 36 h Recommended self-study hours: 124 h
detail.prerequisites: Basic knowledge of Probability theory.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Data Science and Information Engineering
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After completing the course, the student should be able to: describe the slotted multiple-access model, Poisson packet arrival model, and multiple-access protocols to a shared medium. give an account of the properties of mechanisms in protocols for multiple access to a shared medium. formulate assumptions to model and analyze information networks and discuss the validity of these assumptions. perform typical calculations to implement the various algorithms covered in the course.
detail.course_content: Lectures and exercise sessions treat the following topics of Information Networks: Layered network architecture and the OSI model. Slotted multiple-access models and Markov chains. Arrival statistics and the Poisson process. ALOHA system (slotted and unslotted) and stabilization. Splitting algorithms: tree and FCFS algorithms. Performance analysis of multiple-access systems. Carrier sensing: CSMA ALOHA, CSMA/CD, and CSMA/CA. Packet radio networks and IEEE 802.11 protocols. Routing algorithms: minimum-weight spanning trees and shortest-path algorithms.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures, problem-solving classes and a home assignment.
detail.examination: LAB2 Assignment report 2 credits U, G TEN2 Written examination 4 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Internetworking
courses.description: The aim of the course is to give knowledge about internetworking technologies, focusing on the Internet. After completing the course, the student is expected to be able to: Explain how an internet works, which different components are included and why they are included. Give an account of how Internet works on protocol level, what protocols are used, and what functionality these protocols contribute with. Explain how optical fibers, optical components and optical transmitters, amplifiers and receivers work. Explain how optical fiber communication systems work, and their application in the core and access networks of Internet. Describe basic principles of source and channel coding for packet networks, special networks for clouds and Internet-of-things. Perform a simple economic analysis for ISP:s. Set up a local Ethernet network and analyse the traffic in this network.
specialisations: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security

detail.title: Internetworking, 6 credits
detail.name: Internetworking
detail.description: The aim of the course is to give knowledge about internetworking technologies, focusing on the Internet. After completing the course, the student is expected to be able to: Explain how an internet works, which different components are included and why they are included. Give an account of how Internet works on protocol level, what protocols are used, and what functionality these protocols contribute with. Explain how optical fibers, optical components and optical transmitters, amplifiers and receivers work. Explain how optical fiber communication systems work, and their application in the core and access networks of Internet. Describe basic principles of source and channel coding for packet networks, special networks for clouds and Internet-of-things. Perform a simple economic analysis for ISP:s. Set up a local Ethernet network and analyse the traffic in this network.
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 38 h Recommended self-study hours: 122 h
detail.prerequisites: Basic knowledge in computer science
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Information Technology Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master's Programme in Computer Science
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: The aim of the course is to give knowledge about internetworking technologies, focusing on the Internet. After completing the course, the student is expected to be able to: Explain how an internet works, which different components are included and why they are included. Give an account of how Internet works on protocol level, what protocols are used, and what functionality these protocols contribute with. Explain how optical fibers, optical components and optical transmitters, amplifiers and receivers work. Explain how optical fiber communication systems work, and their application in the core and access networks of Internet. Describe basic principles of source and channel coding for packet networks, special networks for clouds and Internet-of-things. Perform a simple economic analysis for ISP:s. Set up a local Ethernet network and analyse the traffic in this network.
detail.course_content: Introduction, Packet switching vs Circuit Switching, the TCP/IP model, the network layer (IPv4 IPv6, routers, switches, DHCP, mobility in LTE, virtual circuits, routing), the transport layer (UDP, TCP), optical fibers, optical transmitters and receivers, passive and active components, wavelength and spatial division multiplexing, lightwave systems, sensor networks, clouds (Clos networks), DNS, multicasting, peer-2-peer, source and channel coding for packet networks (error concealment, interleaving, multiple description coding, layered coding, Shannon bounds).
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures, problem-solving classes and home assignments.
detail.examination: UPG1 Hand-in assignments 1.5 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination. 4.5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

courses.name: Computer Security
courses.description: After completing the course the student is expected to: Comprehend and describe computer security concepts, and design of good security measures for computer systems. Describe standard cryptography tools used in computer and communication systems. Comprehend and describe how to test computer systems for a different number of vulnerabilities. Implement and test different types of cyber-attacks on a simulated environment.
specialisations: Computer Networks, Distributed Systems and Security

detail.title: Computer Security, 6 credits
detail.name: Computer Security
detail.description: After completing the course the student is expected to: Comprehend and describe computer security concepts, and design of good security measures for computer systems. Describe standard cryptography tools used in computer and communication systems. Comprehend and describe how to test computer systems for a different number of vulnerabilities. Implement and test different types of cyber-attacks on a simulated environment.
detail.main_field_of_study: Information Technology, Computer Science and Engineering, Computer Science
detail.education_components: Preliminary scheduled hours: 28 h Recommended self-study hours: 132 h
detail.prerequisites: At least one course in basic computer science, and some knowledge on operating systems and computer communications.
detail.course_offered_for: Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering - International Master of Science in Computer Science and Engineering Master of Science in Applied Physics and Electrical Engineering Master of Science in Computer Science and Software Engineering Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management - International Master of Science in Industrial Engineering and Management Bachelor's Programme in Mathematics Bachelor's Programme in Programming Master's Programme in Computer Science Master's Programme in Mathematics Master's Programme in Cybersecurity
detail.specific_information: Can not be included in degree together with TSIT01 or TDDE53.
detail.intended_learning_outcomes: After completing the course the student is expected to: Comprehend and describe computer security concepts, and design of good security measures for computer systems. Describe standard cryptography tools used in computer and communication systems. Comprehend and describe how to test computer systems for a different number of vulnerabilities. Implement and test different types of cyber-attacks on a simulated environment.
detail.course_content: Security concepts and design: confidentiality, integrity and availability (CIA), risk analysis, threats and vulnerabilities, access control, authentication, biometrics, security models, password handling, operating system security, database security, legal aspects, social engineering. Cryptography-based tools: Symmetric and asymmetric cryptography, cryptanalysis, key generation and length, random number generation, stream and block ciphers, MACs and digital signatures, key management, replay attacks, Kerberos. System testing: Penetration testing, SQL injection, session management, cross-site scripting, poor data validation, proxy servers, cross-site request forgery, ransomware, insecure direct object references.
detail.teaching_and_working_methods: The course consists of lectures, seminars and laboratory work.
detail.examination: LAB1 Laboratory work 1 credits U, G TEN1 Written examination 5 credits U, 3, 4, 5 Grades for examination modules are decided in accordance with the assessment criteria presented at the start of the course.

offerings.name: Computer Security

Describe standard cryptography tools used in computer and communication systems. Comprehend and describe how to test computer systems for a different number of vulnerabilities. Implement and test different types of cyber-attacks on a simulated environment.
